questions.q: How many principal deities make up the Trimurti of Hindu tradition?
questions.explanation: The word Trimurti means 'three forms', comprising Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva.

questions.q: Which deity of the Trimurti is honoured as the creator of the worlds?
questions.explanation: In the Trimurti, Brahma is the creator who brings forth the cosmos (srishti).

questions.q: Which deity of the Trimurti is revered as the preserver of the universe?
questions.explanation: Vishnu fulfils the function of preservation (sthiti), maintaining cosmic order.

questions.q: Which deity of the Trimurti is associated with destruction and transformation?
questions.explanation: Shiva carries out destruction and dissolution (samhara), which renews the cosmic cycle.

questions.q: The Sanskrit term 'Trimurti' is best translated as which of the following?
questions.explanation: 'Trimurti' literally means 'three forms', denoting the threefold divine expression.

questions.q: In which body of Hindu scripture is the concept of the Trimurti most fully elaborated?
questions.explanation: The Trimurti as a unified theological concept is developed chiefly in the Puranas.

questions.q: Which cosmic function is assigned to Brahma within the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Brahma's role in the Trimurti is creation, known in Sanskrit as srishti.

questions.q: According to the Trimurti concept, the three deities are ultimately expressions of which single supreme reality?
questions.explanation: The Trimurti expresses the one Brahman behind the threefold activity of the cosmos.

questions.q: Which of these deities is NOT one of the three members of the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Indra is the king of the devas but is not part of the Trimurti, which is Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva.

questions.q: The flower most often used as an emblem of cosmic creation in Trimurti imagery is which of these?
questions.explanation: The lotus, on which Brahma is depicted seated, is a recurring emblem of creation in Trimurti imagery.

questions.q: In Puranic accounts, Brahma is described as emerging from a lotus that rises from the navel of which deity?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe Brahma seated on a lotus that grows from Vishnu's navel as creation begins.

questions.q: The single three-faced deity often understood as combining Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva is known by which name?
questions.explanation: Dattatreya is depicted in a single three-faced form expressing the unity of the Trimurti.

questions.q: Which Sanskrit term names the cosmic function of preservation associated with Vishnu in the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Sthiti denotes preservation or maintenance, the function attributed to Vishnu.

questions.q: Which Sanskrit term names the cosmic function of dissolution associated with Shiva in the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Samhara denotes destruction or dissolution, the function attributed to Shiva.

questions.q: In Vaishnava tradition, the consort of Vishnu the preserver is which goddess?
questions.explanation: Lakshmi, goddess of fortune and abundance, is the consort of Vishnu.

questions.q: Within the Trimurti, the goddess of learning Saraswati is traditionally regarded as the consort of which deity?
questions.explanation: Saraswati, goddess of knowledge and speech, is the consort of Brahma the creator.

questions.q: The goddess Parvati is traditionally honoured as the consort of which member of the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Parvati is the consort of Shiva, the deity of destruction and transformation.

questions.q: In Hindu iconography, which mount (vahana) is most often associated with Vishnu of the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Vishnu's traditional vahana is the eagle Garuda.

questions.q: Which mount (vahana) is traditionally associated with Shiva of the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Shiva's traditional vahana is the bull Nandi.

questions.q: The swan or goose (Hamsa) is traditionally the mount of which member of the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Brahma's traditional vahana is the swan or goose, the Hamsa.

questions.q: In Vaishnava tradition, Vishnu of the Trimurti is described as reclining upon which cosmic serpent?
questions.explanation: Vishnu reclines upon the thousand-hooded serpent Shesha, also called Ananta, on the cosmic ocean.

questions.q: Which weapon is most characteristically held by Vishnu the preserver in the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Vishnu wields the discus known as the Sudarshana Chakra.

questions.q: The three-pronged weapon, the trishula, is most characteristically associated with which member of the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Shiva is iconographically associated with the trishula, the trident.

questions.q: In Puranic cosmology, a single 'day of Brahma' spanning the life of the worlds is known by which term?
questions.explanation: A kalpa, or day of Brahma, is the vast span across which creation endures before dissolution.

questions.q: Brahma of the Trimurti is frequently depicted with how many faces in classical iconography?
questions.explanation: Brahma is most commonly depicted with four faces, said to look toward the four directions.

questions.q: In the Bhagavad Gita, Vishnu's preserving role is expressed through Krishna's teaching that he descends in every age to restore which principle?
questions.explanation: In the Gita, Krishna declares he is born age after age to restore dharma when righteousness declines.

questions.q: The conch shell, an emblem of the primordial cosmic sound, is most characteristically held by which member of the Trimurti?
questions.explanation: Vishnu holds the conch (shankha), whose sound is linked to the primordial vibration of creation.

questions.q: Brahma, the creator of the Trimurti, is traditionally said to have given voice to which foundational body of sacred texts?
questions.explanation: Tradition holds that the Vedas issued forth from Brahma the creator.

questions.q: The Sanskrit word 'srishti', describing Brahma's role in the Trimurti, refers to which cosmic activity?
questions.explanation: Srishti means creation or bringing-forth, the function attributed to Brahma.

questions.q: In the unity of the Trimurti, the dissolution carried out by Shiva is understood in the Puranas to serve chiefly what purpose?
options: To enable renewal and a fresh cycle of creation
To remove the need for worship
questions.explanation: Shiva's destruction is transformative, clearing the way for renewal and a new cycle of creation.
